back_translation: 'Bezalel and Oholiab and every skilled person to whom the LORD has given wisdom and understanding to know how to do all the work of the service of the sanctuary shall do all that the LORD has commanded.'
Moses called Bezalel and Oholiab and every skilled person in whom the LORD had put wisdom in his heart — everyone whose heart stirred him to come to the work to do it.

They received from before Moses all the contribution that the sons of Israel had brought for the work of the service of the sanctuary, to do it. And they continued bringing to him a freewill offering morning by morning.
All the skilled craftsmen who were doing all the work of the sanctuary came, each man from his work that they were doing,
and they said to Moses, saying, 'The people bring more than enough for the work that the LORD commanded to do.'

Moses gave a command, and they caused a proclamation to pass through the camp, saying, 'Let neither man nor woman do any more work for the contribution of the sanctuary.' So the people were restrained from bringing.
The material was enough for them — for all the work — to do it, and there was more than enough.

All the skilled of heart among the workers of the work made the tabernacle with ten curtains of finely twisted linen, blue, purple, and scarlet yarn; with cherubim, the work of a skilled craftsman, he made them.
The length of one curtain was twenty-eight cubits, and the width four cubits — one measure for all the curtains.
He joined five of the curtains one to another, and five curtains he joined one to another.
He made loops of blue on the edge of the end curtain in the first set; likewise he did on the edge of the outermost curtain in the second set.
Fifty loops he made on the one curtain, and fifty loops he made on the edge of the curtain that was in the second set; the loops were opposite one to another.
He made fifty gold clasps and joined the curtains one to another with the clasps, so that the tabernacle became one.
He made curtains of goats' hair for a tent over the tabernacle; eleven curtains he made of them.
The length of one curtain was thirty cubits, and four cubits the width of one curtain — one measure for the eleven curtains.
He joined five of the curtains by themselves and six of the curtains by themselves.
He made fifty loops on the edge of the outermost curtain in the first set, and fifty loops he made on the edge of the corresponding curtain in the second set.
He made fifty bronze clasps to join the tent together to be one.
He made for the tent a covering of ram skins dyed red, and a covering of fine leather above.
He made the frames for the tabernacle of acacia wood, standing upright.
Ten cubits the length of the frame, and a cubit and a half the width of one frame.
Two tenons for one frame, joined one to another; thus he did for all the frames of the tabernacle.
He made the frames for the tabernacle: twenty frames for the south side.
Forty silver bases he made under the twenty frames — two bases under one frame for its two tenons, and two bases under one frame for its two tenons.
For the second side of the tabernacle, the north side, he made twenty frames.
Their forty silver bases — two bases under one frame, and two bases under one frame.
For the rear of the tabernacle, westward, he made six frames.
He made two frames for the corners of the tabernacle at the rear.
They were coupled from below, and together they were complete to its top to one ring; thus he did for both of them, for the two corners.
There were eight frames and their silver bases — sixteen bases, two bases, two bases under one frame.
He made crossbars of acacia wood — five for the frames of one side of the tabernacle,
and five crossbars for the frames of the second side of the tabernacle, and five crossbars for the frames of the tabernacle at the rear, westward.
He made the middle crossbar to pass through the midst of the frames, from end to end.
He overlaid the frames with gold, and their rings he made of gold — holders for the crossbars — and overlaid the crossbars with gold.
He made the veil of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and finely twisted linen — the work of a skilled craftsman he made it, with cherubim.
He made for it four pillars of acacia and overlaid them with gold; their hooks were of gold, and he cast for them four silver bases.
He made a screen for the entrance of the tent of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and finely twisted linen — the work of an embroiderer,
and its five pillars and their hooks; he overlaid their tops and their bands with gold, and their five bases were bronze.
